title: Field Notes — PlausiDen LLC
description: Engineering notes from PlausiDen engagements. Threat models, architecture decisions, audit-chain proofs, and the practical mathematics of running infrastructure that doesn't rot.

footer.colophon: © 2026 PlausiDen LLC. Reproducible builds. Signed audit chains. No third-party trackers.

sections.title: Engineering notes from the wire.
sections.lede: Anonymized lessons from real engagements. Threat models, architecture decisions, audit-chain proofs, and the mathematics of infrastructure that doesn't rot. No listicles, no top-ten-trends, no clickbait — just what we learned by shipping.

background.alt: Wide editorial photograph evoking engineering notes + craft

sections.caption: Each post traces a real engagement decision — what we tried, what didn't work, what shipped.

sections.text: Anonymized client work, threat-model thinking, and architectural decisions we believe transfer.

items.term: The audit chain that ended a quarterly compliance call
items.definition: Healthcare client; SOC 2 II + HIPAA. We replaced their consultant-prepared evidence binder with a Merkle-chained, Ed25519-signed build report that the auditor accepted on first read. Quarterly meeting now takes 12 minutes instead of 90.

A retail client asked for "AI integration into our stack." We pushed back: which specific workflow, and what's a 30% improvement worth there? The answer was a 90-minute scoping conversation, a 6-week engagement on one workflow (returns triage), and 41% measured throughput gain. Not 'AI integration' — AI on the exact problem AI fits.

items.term: Catching CVSS 4.0 in the supply chain
items.definition: Financial-advisor SaaS, sub-30-person shop. Two transitive Rust deps surfaced as CVSS 4.0 critical in the cargo-deny phase one Tuesday morning. We patched, signed, and shipped before the client's quarterly compliance window. No customer-facing impact; the audit chain proves it.

items.term: Why the runbook is the deliverable
items.definition: Legal client; sole-practitioner office. We engineered their Microsoft 365 + line-of-business stack. The reason they hired us a second time wasn't the engineering — it was the runbook. When a paralegal needed to onboard a new attorney while the partner was on vacation, the runbook covered it. No emergency call to us.

items.term: Threat modeling at the espresso machine
items.definition: Industrial client; food-and-beverage logistics. The OT/IT segmentation engagement opened with: "can you read the temperature off the espresso machines from outside the building?" Yes. Could anyone else? Apparently yes. We segmented the OT network and the cafeteria-grade IoT is no longer reachable from the open internet.

items.term: SLSA-attested SBOMs, the silent way they fail
items.definition: Our own supply-chain CI was emitting zero SBOMs for weeks because the glob path mismatched cargo-cyclonedx's per-crate output. We caught it during a quarterly audit of our own audit chain. We mention this because every shop should audit their own audit chain at the same cadence as their compliance audit.

sections.caption: Field Notes are anonymized but specific. No identifiable client; every detail of the decision is real.

sections.body: Field Notes are anonymized but specific. The names and industries are obscured; the architectural decisions and the math are not.

sections.text: We write for the technical decision-maker who already knows the platforms and the buzzwords and wants to see the work. If you're shopping IT consultancies, the Field Notes give you a clearer view of how we think than any case-study PDF. If you're an internal-IT lead curious how peers structure engagements, we hope they're useful too.

sections.eyebrow: Have a problem worth a Field Note?
sections.title: Bring us in.
sections.lede: Every Field Note starts with a scoped engagement. The notes are anonymized; the engagements are real.
